message:
chore: sync beads and add perf baseline infrastructure

- Add tests/baselines/perf_baseline.json with initial thresholds for
  rendering, buffer, text, input, and E2E performance tracking
- Update bd-1bo1 (Test Coverage Reporting) to in_progress status

The baseline file establishes regression thresholds (20% regression,
10% improvement) for CI performance monitoring.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-19qi","title":"demo_showcase features: pixel rendering showpiece (PixelBuffer + Grayscale + supersampling)","description":"# demo_showcase Features — Pixel Rendering Showpiece (PixelBuffer + Grayscale + Supersampling)

## Purpose

Pixel-level rendering is a unique capability for a terminal UI engine. This bead ensures the demo has an unmistakable “graphics” moment using:

- `PixelBuffer` (RGBA pixels)
- `GrayscaleBuffer` (intensity buffers)
- `OptimizedBuffer::draw_supersample_buffer` (2x2 quadrants)
- `draw_grayscale_buffer_unicode` for sparklines / heatmaps

## Requirements

- The preview panel must render:
  - an animated effect (gradient waves, plasma, noise, or oscilloscope)
  - a small chart (sparkline) driven by deterministic metrics

- The render should be clipped to the panel via scissor.
- Add a semi-transparent UI overlay (labels + toggles) on top of the pixels to demonstrate blending.

## Acceptance Notes

- The preview looks visually impressive (not just static blocks).
- The effect is smooth under the frame pacing system.
- Works in reduced-color terminals with a graceful palette fallback.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- The preview panel renders a clearly animated pixel effect using `PixelBuffer` + supersampling (not just static blocks).
- The panel also renders at least one grayscale-driven visualization (sparkline/heatmap) using `GrayscaleBuffer` helpers.
- Pixel drawing is clipped to the panel’s inner rect via scissor (no bleed).
- A translucent UI overlay (labels/toggles) is drawn over the pixels to visibly prove alpha blending.
- With reduced-color constraints (e.g., `--cap-preset no_truecolor` in headless), the preview still looks good via a graceful palette fallback.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:28:17.567728370Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T03:35:19.700406108Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T03:35:19.700337090Z","close_reason":"Pixel rendering showpiece implemented: animated gradient orb (PixelBuffer with HSV color rotation), FPS sparkline (GrayscaleBuffer with Unicode shade), alpha blending glass overlay. Color fallback handled by ANSI layer.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","features"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-19qi","depends_on_id":"bd-2t3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:24.480066053Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-19qi","depends_on_id":"bd-37p","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:28:17.582950207Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-19qi","depends_on_id":"bd-qn5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:23.922800553Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1al","title":"demo_showcase UI: overlay system (modals + glass + focus capture)","description":"# demo_showcase UI — Overlay System (Modals + Glass + Focus Capture)

## Purpose

The demo app needs multiple overlays that all share the same core behavior:

- semi-transparent “glass” panels layered over the main UI
- dimmed / tinted backdrop
- animated enter/exit (subtle fade / slide)
- input capture (overlay takes focus; underlying UI stops receiving events)
- hit testing for clickable controls inside overlays

This bead builds a reusable overlay foundation so **Help**, **Command Palette**, and **Tour** don’t each reinvent modal behavior.

## Core Requirements

### Rendering

- Overlays render in the final render pass (highest z-order).
- Backdrop uses `push_opacity()` + translucent `fill_rect()` so alpha blending is obvious.
- Overlay body uses:
  - rounded/double border styles
  - soft gradients / subtle noise patterns (procedural) to look “premium”
  - clipped internal scrolling content (scissor stack)

### Input / Focus

- Overlay manager owns:
  - `active: Option<Overlay>` (or a small stack if needed)
  - `focus: OverlayFocus` (which control is focused)
- When an overlay is active:
  - keyboard shortcuts are handled by overlay first
  - mouse hit testing only targets overlay regions
  - optional “click outside to dismiss” behavior

### HitGrid integration

- Overlay rendering must also register hit areas (`Renderer::register_hit_area`).
- Use `Renderer::push_hit_scissor` around overlay internals so hit areas respect clipping.

## Suggested Structure (demo-only)

Inside `src/bin/demo_showcase.rs`:

- `enum Overlay { Help(HelpState), Palette(PaletteState), Tour(TourState) }`
- `struct OverlayManager { active: Option<Overlay>, anim: OverlayAnim, ... }`
- `fn render_overlays(&mut self, ctx: &mut RenderCtx, ...)` called from the render pass bead.

## Acceptance Notes

We consider this “done” when:

- The overlay visuals clearly demonstrate alpha blending + opacity stack (no “flat” look).
- Focus is trapped inside overlays and underlying UI does not react.
- Overlay hit targets work and do not leak outside clipped regions.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Overlays render above the base UI with an obvious dimmed backdrop and glassy modal body using alpha blending.
- Overlays have subtle enter/exit animation (fade/slide) driven by the animation clock.
- When an overlay is active, input is captured: underlying UI does not react.
- Overlay hit testing works for internal controls and respects hit scissoring for clipped regions.
- Help, Command Palette, and Tour reuse the same overlay foundation rather than reimplementing modal behavior.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:23:19.127313380Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:10:23.047477805Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:10:23.047407775Z","close_reason":"Implemented overlay system with OverlayManager, HelpState, PaletteState, TourState. Added draw_pass_overlays with backdrop dimming, glass panels, animated transitions. All three overlay types (Help, Palette, Tour) now render with proper alpha blending and decorative borders.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","ui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-1pd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:31:58.427687351Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-35g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:31:59.010613409Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-3l0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:31:59.560668591Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-c75","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:23:19.140058070Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-po1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:31:57.881744685Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1bdn","title":"EPIC: Memory Safety - Integer Overflow Prevention","description":"## Overview

This epic addresses **CRITICAL MEMORY SAFETY BUGS** discovered during the deep codebase audit. Multiple locations in the buffer and rendering system use unchecked integer arithmetic that can overflow, leading to:

1. **Silent memory corruption** - Wrong indices access wrong data
2. **Out-of-bounds access** - Potential crashes or security issues
3. **Phantom references** - Pool IDs wrap around causing use-after-free scenarios

## Affected Systems

### Buffer Indexing (CRITICAL)
- src/buffer/mod.rs lines 143, 152, 639, 640, 712, 713
- src/buffer/pixel.rs lines 92, 101, 138, 147
- src/renderer/hitgrid.rs line 48

The pattern `y * width + x` is used throughout without overflow checking. For a 10,000x10,000 buffer, overflow occurs at y > 429,496.

### GraphemeId Width Encoding (CRITICAL)
- src/cell.rs lines 68-71
- src/grapheme_pool.rs lines 141-142, 156

Width values >127 are silently truncated because only 7 bits are allocated in the GraphemeId encoding. Width 128 becomes 0, causing incorrect rendering.

### Pool ID Overflow (CRITICAL)  
- src/grapheme_pool.rs line 150-151

The assert() that checks pool ID overflow is REMOVED in release builds. IDs can wrap around the 24-bit boundary, causing pool slot collisions and use-after-free bugs.

## Fix Strategy

1. Use checked/saturating arithmetic for all buffer index calculations
2. Add explicit bounds validation in GraphemeId::new()
3. Replace assert!() with explicit validation that works in release mode
4. Add overflow tests for each affected function

## Impact

Without these fixes, the library has undefined behavior with:
- Large terminal sizes
- Long-running sessions with many unique graphemes
- Complex emoji/grapheme content","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T23:02:28.674325205Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:16:47.460257043Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T23:16:47.460195358Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"bd-1bo1","title":"Infrastructure: Test Coverage Reporting Setup","description":"# Test Coverage & Regression Detection Setup (Consolidated)

Consolidated from bd-1bo1 and bd-2cuu.

## Current State
- NO coverage tooling configured
- No coverage thresholds enforced
- No CI coverage reporting
- No performance regression detection

## Goals
1. Generate and track test coverage
2. Detect performance regressions
3. Fail CI on coverage drops
4. Archive test artifacts

## Tool Selection

**Recommendation: cargo-llvm-cov** for accuracy and cross-platform support.

Alternatives considered:
- cargo-tarpaulin (easiest, Linux-focused)
- grcov (Mozilla's tool)

## Coverage Configuration

### .cargo/config.toml
```toml
[llvm-cov]
exclude-patterns = ["tests/*", "benches/*"]
```

### Coverage Thresholds

| Module | Target | Rationale |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| Overall | ≥ 80% | Good baseline |
| src/color.rs | ≥ 95% | Core algorithm, must be correct |
| src/buffer/ | ≥ 90% | Critical path |
| src/renderer/ | ≥ 85% | Complex but testable |
| src/text/ | ≥ 85% | Well-tested already |
| src/terminal/ | ≥ 75% | Some FFI limits |
| src/input/ | ≥ 90% | Well-tested already |
| New code | 100% | All new code must be tested |

## CI Workflow

### .github/workflows/coverage.yml
```yaml
name: Coverage
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  coverage:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Install coverage tools
        run: cargo install cargo-llvm-cov
        
      - name: Run tests with coverage
        run: cargo llvm-cov --all-features --workspace --lcov --output-path lcov.info
        
      - name: Check thresholds
        run: cargo llvm-cov --fail-under-lines 80
        
      - name: Generate HTML report
        run: cargo llvm-cov --html --output-dir coverage
        
      - name: Upload to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
        with:
          files: lcov.info
          
      - name: Upload HTML report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: coverage-report
          path: coverage/
```

## Performance Regression Detection

### Benchmark Tracking
```yaml
- name: Run benchmarks
  run: cargo bench --bench buffer -- --save-baseline ci

- name: Compare to main
  run: cargo bench --bench buffer -- --baseline main
  continue-on-error: true
```

### Metrics to Track
- Buffer operations (cells/sec)
- Diff computation (time)
- ANSI generation (bytes/sec)
- Text insertion (ops/sec)
- Alpha blending (ops/sec)

## Test Artifacts

### On Failure Archive
```yaml
- uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
  if: failure()
  with:
    name: test-artifacts
    path: |
      target/test-artifacts/
      target/llvm-cov/
```

### Artifact Contents
- JSONL logs from E2E tests
- Buffer dumps
- ANSI captures
- Stack traces
- Coverage HTML report

## Local Development

```bash
# Generate HTML coverage report
cargo llvm-cov --html

# View immediately
cargo llvm-cov --open

# Check thresholds locally
cargo llvm-cov --fail-under-lines 80
```

## Files to Create
- .github/workflows/coverage.yml
- codecov.yml (Codecov configuration)
- COVERAGE.md - workflow documentation

## Acceptance Criteria
- Coverage report generated in CI
- Thresholds enforced (fail on drop)
- Badge in README
- Local workflow documented
- Performance baseline established
- Artifacts captured on failure","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:46:48.952771258Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T20:24:33.372357566Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1bo1","depends_on_id":"bd-1rfq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:46:48.958594575Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1bo1","depends_on_id":"bd-2e6f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:49:35.382125974Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1bo1","depends_on_id":"bd-h3gn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:49:36.085480007Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1by","title":"demo_showcase runtime: robust cleanup + panic hook (restore terminal on crash)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Robust Cleanup + Panic Hook

## Purpose

Even though `Renderer` and `Terminal` try to restore state on Drop, a panic can still leave the user’s terminal in a bad state (especially if aborting).

This bead adds belt-and-suspenders cleanup so the demo is “safe to run”.

## Approach

- Install a panic hook that:
  - attempts best-effort cleanup
  - prints a helpful message (`reset` / `stty sane`)

Important:

- Keep it minimal and avoid introducing dependencies.
- Do not swallow panic info; chain to the original hook.

## Scope

This is demo-only code in `src/bin/demo_showcase.rs`.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:
- [ ] Panic hook attempts terminal cleanup
- [ ] Original panic hook is preserved
- [ ] Crash instructions are printed clearly","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:11:17.714696250Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T04:37:16.145349704Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T04:37:16.145257883Z","close_reason":"Implemented panic hook with terminal cleanup: ANSI reset sequences, termios restoration, and recovery instructions","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","resilience","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1by","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:11:17.733296335Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1by","depends_on_id":"bd-z1b","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:12:31.148134484Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1c6","title":"[EPIC] Expanded Examples Suite","description":"# Epic: Expanded Examples Suite

## Background & Motivation

OpenTUI currently has only two examples:
1. `hello.rs` - Basic buffer creation and text drawing (doesn't even render to terminal)
2. `editor.rs` - Full interactive editor demo

There's a significant gap between these. Users need:
- Simple, focused examples showing one feature at a time
- Progressive complexity to learn the library
- Real-world patterns they can copy into their projects
- Examples demonstrating all major features

## Goals

1. Provide examples covering all major OpenTUI features
2. Order examples by complexity (beginner → advanced)
3. Each example should be self-contained and focused on one concept
4. Include inline comments explaining key patterns
5. Examples should be runnable and visually interesting

## Proposed Example Structure

```
examples/
├── 01_hello_terminal.rs     # Basic terminal setup + text output
├── 02_colors.rs             # RGBA colors, hex, HSV, blending
├── 03_styles.rs             # Bold, italic, underline, inverse
├── 04_drawing.rs            # Boxes, lines, fill_rect
├── 05_scissoring.rs         # Scissor clipping demonstration
├── 06_opacity.rs            # Opacity stacks and transparency
├── 07_input.rs              # Keyboard and mouse input handling
├── 08_animation.rs          # Simple animation loop
├── 09_text_buffer.rs        # TextBuffer and styled segments
├── 10_edit_buffer.rs        # Editable text with cursor
├── 11_highlighting.rs       # Syntax highlighting (after highlight impl)
├── 12_panels.rs             # Multiple panels/windows pattern
├── 13_menu.rs               # Dropdown/popup menu pattern
├── 14_progress.rs           # Progress bars and spinners
├── 15_dashboard.rs          # Complex multi-widget dashboard
└── editor.rs                # (Existing) Full editor demo
```

## Design Principles

- **Progressive disclosure** - Start simple, build complexity
- **One concept per example** - Don't overwhelm with features
- **Copy-paste friendly** - Code should work with minimal modification
- **Visual appeal** - Make examples look good so users are inspired
- **Commented thoroughly** - Explain the "why" not just the "what"

## Non-Goals

- Complete application examples (that's what the editor demo is for)
- Platform-specific examples
- Integration with external crates (keep dependencies minimal)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] At least 10 new example files
- [ ] Examples build and run without errors
- [ ] README or doc comments explain what each example demonstrates
- [ ] New user can understand OpenTUI by reading examples in order
- [ ] Examples showcase features not obvious from API docs

## Dependencies

Most examples can be created immediately. The highlighting example (11) depends on the syntax highlighting epic completion.

## Estimated Scope

~1500-2000 LOC across all examples.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T01:09:23.805421096Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T17:55:13.555654383Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T17:55:13.555508939Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["epic","examples"]}
 {"id":"bd-1c6.1","title":"Example: Basic terminal setup and rendering","description":"# Example: Basic Terminal Setup and Rendering

## Purpose

This is the true "hello world" - demonstrating the minimum code to:
1. Create a Renderer
2. Draw something to the buffer
3. Present it to the terminal
4. Handle cleanup

The existing `hello.rs` doesn't actually render to terminal - it just creates a buffer and prints dimensions. This example fills that gap.

## Code Structure

```rust
//! Example 01: Basic Terminal Rendering
//! 
//! This example demonstrates the fundamental OpenTUI rendering loop:
//! 1. Create a Renderer (enters alternate screen, hides cursor)
//! 2. Get the back buffer and draw to it
//! 3. Present the frame (writes to terminal)
//! 4. Wait for user input to exit
//! 5. Renderer drops and restores terminal automatically

use opentui::{Renderer, Rgba, Style};
use std::io::{self, Read};

fn main() -> io::Result<()> {
    // Create renderer with terminal dimensions
    // This automatically:
    // - Enters alternate screen (saves current terminal content)
    // - Hides the cursor
    // - Enables raw mode (direct key input)
    let mut renderer = Renderer::new(80, 24)?;
    
    // Clear the buffer with a dark background
    let buffer = renderer.buffer();
    buffer.clear(Rgba::from_hex("#1a1a2e").unwrap());
    
    // Draw centered text
    let title = "Welcome to OpenTUI!";
    let x = (80 - title.len() as u32) / 2;
    buffer.draw_text(x, 10, title, Style::fg(Rgba::from_hex("#00ff88").unwrap()).with_bold());
    
    let subtitle = "Press any key to exit...";
    let x = (80 - subtitle.len() as u32) / 2;
    buffer.draw_text(x, 12, subtitle, Style::fg(Rgba::from_hex("#888888").unwrap()));
    
    // Present the frame (diff-based, efficient)
    renderer.present()?;
    
    // Wait for any key
    let _ = io::stdin().read(&mut [0u8]);
    
    // Renderer::drop() automatically:
    // - Exits alternate screen (restores previous content)
    // - Shows the cursor
    // - Disables raw mode
    
    Ok(())
}
```

## Key Concepts Demonstrated

1. **Renderer lifecycle** - Create → draw → present → drop
2. **Alternate screen** - Terminal content preserved
3. **Automatic cleanup** - RAII pattern with Drop trait
4. **Basic drawing** - `clear()` and `draw_text()`
5. **Color creation** - `Rgba::from_hex()`
6. **Style application** - `Style::fg()` and `.with_bold()`

## Testing

- Run example: `cargo run --example 01_hello_terminal`
- Verify text appears centered on dark background
- Verify pressing a key exits cleanly
- Verify terminal is restored after exit

## File to Create

- `examples/01_hello_terminal.rs`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Example compiles and runs
- [ ] Output is visually appealing
- [ ] Terminal restores properly on exit
- [ ] Code has thorough comments
- [ ] Demonstrates minimum viable OpenTUI usage","notes":"## Testing Requirements

Per bd-1c6.11 (Examples Validation Test Suite):
- Example MUST compile with `cargo build --example 01_hello_terminal`
- Run with: `cargo run --example 01_hello_terminal`
- Verify terminal restores correctly on exit (no corruption)
- Test on both light and dark terminal backgrounds

## User Experience

This is the FIRST example users will see. It must:
- Work flawlessly on first try
- Have clear, educational comments
- Demonstrate OpenTUI's "just works" philosophy
- Leave the terminal in pristine condition on exit","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T01:09:42.262261577Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T12:41:42.829409444Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T12:41:42.829391049Z","close_reason":"Added 01_hello_terminal example with renderer flow and README entry","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["beginner","examples"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1c6.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1c6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:52:23Z","created_by":"import"}]}

@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
